sentences.text: The capture was 11 May 1960, and I think it was about 8:00
20 in the evening.
Yeah.
One of the-- one of the-- within your conversations with Eichmann, he was talking about how he loved children.
And he had said that he loved all children.
He loved, for sure-- he-- he didn't have to show me, because I saw how he loved his child when he-- when my observation there, going into the place-- I was sure that he likes his family.
He didn't mind the money, didn't mind-- be famous or not.
He lived in a very modest way.
He worked with Mercedes-Benz at the time in Buenos Aires.
So what he-- I think-- so, he loved the children.
That's for sure.
So the question was, what kind of children he liked-- he loved.
For his children, he for sure loved-- his family he liked.
But what he talked about when I told him-- when we talked later, he said, about the Jews, look, I didn't-- I didn't hate Jews.
In a way, he even loved them.
I said, what-- why do you care?
What-- how you mean about-- they even knew a few words of-- of Hebrew-- [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- and also one word, of course-- [SPEAKING HEBREW].
That was something.
They were taking very much.
And I even was in-- in Israel. 1936 he came.
And he came to Haifa.
In a way, he didn't go farther.
I think the English stopped him or something like this.
But he was-- and he, like a good German, has his job.
He prepared himself for the job very well.
Because he had to understand the Jews, to know their language, their mentality, and all these kind of thing, not because-- it's only for one reason, how that he will kill them.
They will send them to the concentration camp with-- how do you say?
With no question.
And he used a lot-- how do you say?-- the Jewish people.
Because, you know, it's like Kastner-- Kastner in Hungary, other people, you know, to-- he understood that he has to make kind of relation.
There would be no resistance.
That was the new kind of murderer, that behind a desk he could do much more, in any case, and [INAUDIBLE] weapon.

And that was the first time that through-- under his desk, he sent so many Ukraines, and manipulate so many things and-- and-- and succeeded in-- in concentrate so many people, six million of Jews and maybe another six million of other people, in the concentration or killing and all this kind of thing.
I think even more for him.

For him, he-- he saw that his job is the most important thing, that he has to make it, fulfill it to the end, because this was the order of his boss, of-- of Hitler and Himmler and later Kaltenbrunner and the others.
So that was the job that he has to do, and he was kind of-- to show that he fulfilled as much as possible, he fulfilled his job.
He did what he can.
That was the guy.
That was the man.
For him, Hitler was God and, in order, was the most important-- his bible.
And no question about it.
And maybe people ask me, what did you learn about it?
What-- how-- what-- what did you-- what kind of moral you got from all of this happening, that you-- after the capture of Eichmann?
I said, two things-- one, nobody's your God.
And-- and an order is not-- is not granted.
You don't do things, only through your conscience.
If somebody orders you, in the army, to fight somebody else, that's something else, because you give the chances to the other side exactly like you have, two armies for this.
You don't know whom you do.
But to go against children, women, all this, innocent people-- they can't even-- how do you say?-- resist or something.
Nobody can do it.
And even if you want to save your life, you can't do it.
And to fulfill an order?
What is it?
And he found it, that the Reich is-- it's not less important or maybe more important.
Because the Jews-- it was said that the Jews are-- oh, how do you say?
The-- because of them, everything happened.
And what happened even to the German is because of the Jews.
And that's what happened.
In the beginning, it was the Crystal Night, you know?
Then was the judenrein.
And he said to me, look, what do you want to fuss?
We said only in the beginning judenrein, clean the-- clean the Jews from Germany and later from the occupied land.
And who-- you blame me?
You blame us?
And who want to take them?
Was any nation around the world who accepted them?
You remember the ship that goes around?
And nobody accepted it.
Nobody accepted, because they thought is a little-- this is kind of a little war between the German and the Jews.
They didn't understand that the mother is a mother and it could happen also-- and then it became stronger and stronger.
It became like that the English being involved and then the American and then-- and then nobody-- nobody said they're coming in.
And then Israel, the English didn't let even after the war the survivors in.
So it was judenrein-- didn't help.
What could we do?
We started to make-- put the concentration, make a concentration.
The concentration were-- were full.
What could we do again?
You have to shoot.
And shooting was not enough, so we came with the gas.
And so on and on.
And he explained, very-- how do you say?-- systematically, it has to be done.
There was no other way.
And he sometimes-- that he was so loyal, that he worked.
He didn't run away after the war.
He waited at the last moment.
He'd forgotten that he talked to a Jew, that he shows how much he was, you know, loyal to the-- and that's exactly what he was.
He-- he-- he was loyal to the-- to the system.
You talk to him, and you try to find out things, and you see, like we are-- we are together and separated, like a wall of glass, as I described, is between me and him.
I talk, and he talk.
And nobody understand the other.
I can't understand how he talks about children, small children.
And he gave a good answer about all this, an explanation.
No way you could accept it.
But that was the man.
And I was not-- my duty, what to do to him-- I knew it's going to be a trial, and he has to be sent.
I don't know what will-- they will do with him.
And then I made him exercises.
I put on him the dress-- dress, you know.
And suddenly in one of the evenings, I got-- I knew, because we didn't know what we're going to do with him.
There's going to be an airplane from El Al that will being him to Israel.
And that was a new thing.
We didn't know.
And that was-- that was-- that happened because it was 150 years of the independence, and we caught him at that time.
It was accident.
And Abba Eban came, the head of the team, to celebrate, you know, the independence, to have a good relationship with-- with Argentina.
And suddenly, when we kept him, we say-- and he didn't know what we were going to bring, was never told the purpose of coming of this airplane.
And then the whole thing was to bring him to the airplane.
And Eichmann-- and we-- and when we brought him there, he was very angry that he was left behind.
The airplane left with Eichmann, leaving him behind.
That was very-- I think that Israel and-- and how do you call him?-- Abba Eban have been very angry at each other, because he made him as a bait, you know, for-- for bringing the airplane.
Did Abba Eban-- Abba Eban did not know?
Did not know-- no.
And that was a terrible thing.
And then we broke relationship.
And there was a big thing about six-- I don't know-- about one year, had a lot of problems between the two countries, because they said we have done something against the law.
We kidnapped somebody as a-- their resident.
The-- how do you say?
And we have to bring him back.
There was a lot of talking and-- and only-- only very later-- how do you say?
They accepted each other.
The relationship started again.
So-- we used him.
We used this airplane to bring over-- over Eichmann.
Now, what want to say is when I disguised him-- I didn't know.
And I disguised him at the time I knew about the airplane.
I didn't know-- how do you say?
In the beginning, we didn't know how we're going to bring him.
When I disguised him, put the hat on him, all this, he said-- he looked in the mirror and said, oh, I like this uniform.
I didn't put the Star of David because I wanted beginning to see-- he liked-- suddenly he felt himself at home with the uniform and everything and all this.
And I gave him-- how do you say?
And then I put the Star of David.
He said, well, what is this?
And I said, now, OK.
I didn't tell him there's going to be a steward, you know, Israeli steward and all this.
But after they put a picture and everything and we make him a steward-- and that was the problem that, I don't know, somehow the head of the Secret Service brought a piece of paper.
It's written there, [INAUDIBLE] and all this.
It didn't-- it didn't succeed.
It's not so important, the whole thing-- I didn't understand what's so important that he will sign on the paper, I'm ready to go to Jerusalem, willingly.
And if he wouldn't sign, what now do it?

But Israel thought, if I sign the paper, if you sign the paper, if the police will come, you show him that he accepted, show him he's arrested, chain on his foot, and he willingly-- it's big nonsense in my mind.
But still, you know, it's an order, an order.
But for me, it was something else.
I wanted to convince him to go-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to accept this signing, because it was like an opening, a new window that I can help.
Maybe he will tell me where's Mengele and all this if he-- so-- He all the time said he likes wine.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]-- I like wine, red wine.
For me a big thing, so I went one night, very-- I didn't tell my team, because they would kill me that I even talked to him.
They would kill me.
And I said, OK.
I brought this win.
I made-- brought some music.
It was like flamenco.
And very silently, I gave him the wine, drink.
He said, why do why you do it for me?
And I said, oh, for you or not.
I don't think that you are responsible for all what happened in Germany.
Responsibility you share.
But still, I don't have-- you know, there will be a trial, and they will decide what you have done.
But if you like wine, I've brought you some wine.
And we lit a cigarette.
We drink.
I said, I won't tell you this paper-- you see, you've seen it.
You don't want to sign it.
Sign it or not sign it, you're going to Jerusalem.
But I think it's good for you, for your children and all this you will stand in Jerusalem and give you-- you view of what happened and who is guilty or not guilty.
And you sign it.
I leave you the paper.
You decide.
You want to sign, OK.
For me, it was not important.
And he signed it.
I said, I'll do it.
I said, you know what?
I will come to visit you in Jerusalem when you are there.
He said, really?
OK.
I ask him about Mengele, Bohman, others.
And he really cried and said, no I don't know.
I don't where they are.
And I believed him, because what happened to the Germans, they had been so frightened to tell that they are-- they are near him.
Everybody ran away, because Eichmann was the most wanted Nazi at the time, that everybody looked up to him.
So any other Nazi ran away.
And we almost, you know-- it's in the book.
Two weeks ago-- two weeks before, Eichmann left the place where we looked after him.
Thanks God he left, because no-- no way we could capture two at the same time and we could coordinate it in the right way.
You capture too much-- I have to tell you--
No, then-- then the doctor came, and he gave him an injection.
He said, I don't need it.
OK, but still-- gave him an injection that he will feel like, you know, a drunk man-- not he couldn't answer your question right.
But he would be like dizzy, you know.
And that was the-- as I said, the independence of Argentina.
The day everybody drank and was happy and music and all this.
So it was.

And we already-- the team of El Al was coming and going, coming and going, coming and going into the-- with our people, dressed as a team, gave the guards,that they will know them, something to drink, a bottle here, a bottle there, and a present- from Israel.
So it became that if somebody will come in, they don't ask him too much of identities and all this.
And we decide that we put him between, you know, two El Al people and others will be-- and then he will cross, you know, into-- go into the area of the airport and will go into the airplane.
And that's what really happened that night.
I was not with them.
I didn't go there.
Because somebody has to stay behind.
I stayed behind and-- with other two people there to give back the cars, you know.
And in the it's not only what you do.
It's not like in war.
You kill, shoot and you leave everything, all the evidence there.
In Secret Service, normally you have to close things right.
There will be no evidence, who did it, where it was.
Wouldn't-- how do you say?-- later kill some Jews because they helped it and all this.
We have to clean.
And we stayed another-- about another month in South America to cross back.
There was an earthquake at that time in Chile.
Thousands of people died.
It was very difficult to get airplanes.
We stayed another month, about more, later.
Yes.
After-- in the moment when we captured him and we brought him into the villa-- we'd been close.
We didn't do anything, only monitoring the radio to know what's happening.
We had outside people that would tell is what the police do, all the children and all this.
But still, not everything, you know, really came to a conclusion for sure, is that they are going to use the police that were pro-German a lot.
And they will use-- how do you say?
They will try the-- the-- how do you call it?
The context with that they have the pro-Nazis, a lot there.
And they have themselves.
They after their father.
And we knew that already, whatever will happen, somebody's looking after him.
And that was our fear also when the El Al came in.
They weren't coming to the conclusion that El Al came for some-- something other, other thing.
So all this was taken into consideration.
That's why he was disguised, that even they see him, they wouldn't know that he's the guy.
That was the reason for disguising him.
Even the disguise also was a-- it wouldn't-- let's say it was-- it wouldn't work like that we can bring him into the airport.
After bringing somewhere else, we are no going to leave him like this, again start the story.
So, that was the reason that-- that-- how do you say?-- that the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was the-- Well, just about the sons who-- who-- who--
Ah, that the-- that was really the disguise and the guard of him, because we had been afraid that the family would be there.
And that's what happened, really, that-- I don't remember exactly the timing, but they came later.
And they looked after him, but the airplane already left.
So they came--
Yeah.
I-- I think so, that they came-- I-- I'm not sure on a half hour or this.
I don't remember now.
But I know they came later, and it was too late.
Or they came when the airplane already left.
And-- and I remember I stayed there with the girl, with somebody else.
And-- and we cleaned everything.
We tried to give back the couch and everything, and all this, we cleaned.

And then our way-- on our way back-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember I decided to write the book about my family, about myself, as the book you have seen, And I said I couldn't finish it will I will know exactly-- how do you say-- where I was born and-- not born-- where I lived, and my parents and all this.
And I got it through the taxi driver.
And then after one year-- I've forgotten, really, about the process.
I was sure somebody would invite me.
And I come to Jerusalem.
I see a line.
I said, what is it?
What they sell here?
You know, at that time, I looked, and I see-- I said, look, this is the [GERMAN], I think they call it.
They're going to process of Eichmann.
I said, oh my God.
I promised him that I will visit him in Jerusalem, and I'll be there, and I've forgotten about it.
And I bought my way.
I buy the ticket, everything, you know, I have to-- all this.
And I said, oh, hell, they didn't invite me even to this-- to the trial.
I walked in-- I walked inside.
I-- saw the booth.
And I-- and the people, they'll say-- well, what are you standing?
Wait.
Wait.
Oh-- security and all.
And suddenly, he came in, and he saw me.
And we nodded, and he didn't answer a question.
And that was the time I say I said I paid my visit.
And I left the place.
Said, what will I hear here?
I heard already enough.
And I got my conclusions.
And then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I said I have to write the book.
And I got also the other information.
And I know only that a friend of mine, that he was-- [INAUDIBLE]-- that he went to the hanging-- hanging, when he was hanged.
And he said to me, that only thing what he said is, 'To hell with you.
Long live Germany.'
That's the only one, that you couldn't change him.
He never believed in-- he always believed what he's done was right and always believed that the Jews have been-- have been these people that have to be killed, because-- how do you say?
It was good for Germany.
That's my story.
That's sounds fascinating.
That was in 1962 that he was hanged?
He was hanged in the end of 1961, '62, beginning of '62.
One year there was the full-- it was one of the biggest trial.
I think all the newspapers around the world was there-- were there, and it was really something.
I think it brought most of-- a lot of evidence.
It gives a lot of news.
It gives-- also portrayed the-- the-- how do you say?-- who was one of their leaders, you know, who was one of these people who was really responsible for this final solution.
He was the one that I visited later, showed my paintings in the Wannsee Conference, became a museum.
And he was the one who gave the menu for the-- like the menu for the-- for the--
Huh?
For Wannsee Conference, and he was there and deciding the-- about the final solution.
So--
Mostly I lecture in America, not Germany.
But I've done a few.
Yes.
For thousands of children.
Yeah.
One time I came to a rating that was-- how many believe about if it's enough, what they've done for the Jews, about the Holocaust or not.
And there have been about 66%.
Yeah, what Germany-- 66% said that's enough.
And about 34% said, no, we have to do much more for the-- for the Jews still, for the survivors.
And when they ask me, what do you say about it?
I came-- you know, just as a visitor for another program.
And I said, you asked me a question that we can't answer it, no you and not me.
And I think all the rating is wrong.
Because you asked me something that we can-- we are responsible to do this or that or to erase what happened or not.
It is-- when I take a secretary, I look at her file.
In the file is written what she has done.
And if I see somebody killed somebody, I take her as a secretary?
No.
Well, it's very important for me what she has done in her life.
And the German people had a file.
It's written there-- they killed many, many people, and among them, about six million of Jews.
And among them is one million of children.
You want me to now to make the rating if it's OK, if you paid enough or not?
You paid is for the painting, for the houses, for the material things.
You didn't paint for life-- you didn't pay for life.
All your money, what you give, is not worth the one child.
So your rating is something that is-- it's nonsense.
Because one child of you will come up on day and will say, what-- what happened in Germany, in this world?
Why people say you killed-- how'd you do it?
Now, I said, look, gentlemen.
When you are born, you start a line.
Behind you is the past.
You step on the present.
And-- and-- in front is the future.
So whatever you leave behind you is, what you left, is the millions of people that you killed.
And you can't just take a scissor and to cut it off.
It's all one line.
And now the question is only one thing.
What you can do is, if you can balance this line.
If the new generation, the children-- and also what's happened in the future, if you've justified-- if you can balance the situation, that you can show that you will never happen again.
And that you have to show.
No way you can ask about rating what happened in the past.
The past is a very dark past.
The future, it depends on you.
